name: Turk Murphy
abstract: Melvin Edward Alton "Turk" Murphy (December 16, 1915 – May 30, 1987) was an American trombonist and bandleader, who played traditional and Dixieland jazz.

works_created.t: Bay City (The Turk Murphy Jazz Band Theme)

about_quotes.speaker: Bob Schulz
about_quotes.evidence: I came out and joined a gentleman by the name of Turk Murphy in San Francisco.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bob Schulz interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 15, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Bernard Steinau
about_quotes.evidence: Patty being more forward than I, didn't require being very forward, introduced herself and me to Turk. And we became really quite friendly with the band, particularly with Turk and with Bob Short and his wife of that particular period. And visited, did some visiting back and forth throughout the yea

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Allred
about_quotes.evidence: I remember Turk Murphy, I sat in with Turk’s band out in San Francisco.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bill Allred interviewed by Monk Rowe, Orlando, Florida, February 24, 2017

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Mead
about_quotes.evidence: I mean we were much more listening to the stuff that was going on in New York, so Turk Murphy and Kid Ory and stuff like that, not that we didn’t like it we just didn’t deal with that very much.
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Mead interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, June 1, 2006

about_quotes.speaker: Avakian George
about_quotes.evidence: Turk Murphy lived in San Francisco. We were old friends from my seeing him when I went overseas and he was just about to go into the Navy with the entire Lu Watters band...
about_quotes.doc_title: Avakian George — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Ed Polcer
about_quotes.evidence: if you go out to the west coast and hear the Turk Murphy offshoots... you have the California banjo-tuba-bass on Turk Murphy, and you have all these offshoots
about_quotes.doc_title: Ed Polcer interviewed by Monk Rowe, Tarrytown, New York, October 31, 1998
